description: Golden set for software testing assistant: 42 cases (M4 expansion; M1 started with 10 seeds).

cases.question: What is the difference between verification and validation in software testing?
cases.reference_answer: Verification checks whether the product was built correctly against specifications (reviews, static analysis, unit tests against design). Validation checks whether the right product was built for user needs (system tests, UAT, acceptance criteria). In short: verification = are we building it right; validation = are we building the right thing.
must_not_include: verification and validation are the same
cases.source: Core ISTQB-style concept; common interview question
cases.notes: Expect clear contrast, not a long textbook dump.

cases.question: What is a flaky test, and why is it harmful in CI?
cases.reference_answer: A flaky test sometimes passes and sometimes fails without intentional product changes. Common causes include timing/race conditions, shared mutable state, external dependencies, and non-deterministic data. Flakes erode trust in CI: teams ignore red builds, hide real regressions, and waste time on re-runs instead of fixing root causes.
must_not_include: flaky means the feature is always broken

cases.question: How would you prioritize test cases when you have limited time before a release?
cases.reference_answer: Prioritize by risk and user impact: critical user journeys, high-severity areas, recent code changes, and historically buggy modules. Prefer smoke and high-value regression over exhaustive edge coverage. Document what was deferred and why so residual risk is visible to stakeholders. Time-box exploratory testing on the riskiest new behaviour.
must_not_include: skip testing if time is short
cases.notes: Good answers sound like a QA lead, not a checklist bot.

cases.question: What is the Page Object Model in UI automation, and what problem does it solve?
cases.reference_answer: Page Object Model (POM) represents each page or component as a class that encapsulates locators and user actions. Tests call high-level methods instead of raw selectors. This reduces duplication, isolates UI churn to one place, and makes tests more readable and maintainable as the product UI changes.
must_not_include: one giant class for the whole app is best

cases.question: What should an API automated test typically assert beyond HTTP status 200?
cases.reference_answer: Assert response body structure and values (schema or contract), important business fields, error codes and messages on negative paths, headers when relevant (content-type, auth), and idempotency/side effects for write operations. Also cover unauthorized and invalid-input cases. Status 200 alone can hide wrong payloads.

cases.question: What is the difference between severity and priority of a defect?
cases.reference_answer: Severity describes the technical impact of the bug on the system (crash vs cosmetic). Priority describes business urgency of the fix (fix now vs later). A low-severity typo on the checkout button can be high priority; a severe crash in a rare admin tool may be lower priority. QA usually proposes severity; product/engineering often sets priority.
must_not_include: severity and priority are always identical

cases.question: How is testing an LLM-powered feature different from classical regression testing?
cases.reference_answer: LLM features are non-deterministic: the same prompt can yield different wording, so exact string asserts are brittle. Failures include hallucinations, unsafe content, weak retrieval, and silent quality drift after prompt or model changes. Evaluation uses golden sets, graded metrics (e.g. faithfulness, relevancy), sampling, and thresholds rather than only pass/fail UI steps. Cost and latency become quality attributes. Human review still matters for edge cases.
must_not_include: LLM testing is identical to unit testing strings
only manual vibe checks are needed
cases.notes: Core differentiator case for AI QA roles.

cases.question: Why run automated tests in CI (for example GitHub Actions) on every pull request?
cases.reference_answer: CI runs the same suite in a clean environment on every change, catching regressions early before merge. It enforces a shared quality gate, reduces “works on my machine” issues, and provides artifacts (logs, HTML reports, traces) for debugging. For teams, CI makes quality visible and repeatable rather than relying on ad-hoc local runs.
must_not_include: only run tests once a year

cases.question: When should you not automate a test case?
cases.reference_answer: Avoid automating one-off checks, highly unstable UI with no ROI, pure visual aesthetics better suited to design review, or cases that change every sprint with no stable oracle. Prefer automation for repetitive, high-risk, stable flows. Exploratory and usability testing stay largely manual. Automating everything can create a brittle suite that costs more to maintain than it saves.

cases.question: How would you design negative API tests for a login endpoint?
cases.reference_answer: Cover missing fields, wrong password, unknown user, empty body, malformed JSON, SQL/script injection strings, locked or disabled accounts if applicable, and rate-limit behaviour if specified. Assert correct HTTP status (401/400/403/429 as designed), stable error codes/messages, no stack traces or sensitive data in responses, and that no session token is issued on failure. Keep tests data-driven for maintainability.
cases.notes: Aligns with Project 1 negative cases mindset.

cases.question: What is regression testing?
cases.reference_answer: Regression testing re-runs selected tests after changes to check that existing behaviour still works. It protects against unintended side effects when code, config, or dependencies change. Suites are often automated and run in CI for fast feedback.

cases.question: What is smoke testing?
cases.reference_answer: Smoke testing is a shallow, fast set of checks that the build is stable enough for deeper testing. It covers critical launch paths (start app, login, core navigation). If smoke fails, further testing is usually blocked until the build is fixed.

cases.question: What is a test oracle?
cases.reference_answer: A test oracle is the mechanism that decides whether an observed result is correct. Oracles can be expected values, prior system behaviour, specifications, or human judgment. Without a clear oracle, automation cannot reliably pass or fail a check.

cases.question: What is boundary value analysis?
cases.reference_answer: Boundary value analysis designs tests at the edges of input ranges where defects often cluster, such as min, max, just inside, and just outside limits. It complements equivalence partitioning by focusing on edges rather than only representative middle values.

cases.question: What is equivalence partitioning?
cases.reference_answer: Equivalence partitioning groups inputs that should behave the same into classes, then tests one or few values per class. This reduces redundant cases while keeping coverage of distinct behaviours, including invalid partitions for negative tests.
must_not_include: test every possible input value always

cases.question: What is risk-based testing?
cases.reference_answer: Risk-based testing allocates effort where failure impact or likelihood is highest. Teams rank features by business criticality, change frequency, and defect history, then deepen tests on top risks first. Lower-risk areas get lighter coverage when time is limited.

cases.question: How do you decide the test pyramid balance for a web app?
cases.reference_answer: Prefer many fast unit tests, fewer integration tests, and a thin UI end-to-end layer. UI tests are valuable for critical journeys but are slower and flakier, so keep them focused. APIs and services should carry most contract and business-rule checks below the UI.

cases.question: How would you plan testing for a feature behind a feature flag?
cases.reference_answer: Test both flag-off and flag-on behaviour, including default state for existing users. Cover rollout percentage edge cases if applicable, configuration mistakes, and cleanup when the flag is removed. Automate critical paths for both states and verify analytics or logging still make sense.
must_not_include: test only when flag is on

cases.question: What is the difference between unit tests and end-to-end tests?
cases.reference_answer: Unit tests verify small units of code in isolation with fast feedback. End-to-end tests exercise full user flows through the real stack and catch integration issues but are slower and more brittle. Both belong in a balanced strategy.

cases.question: Why is waiting for fixed sleeps a bad practice in UI automation?
cases.reference_answer: Hard-coded sleeps slow the suite and still fail under load variance. Prefer explicit waits for conditions such as element visible, network idle, or text present. Condition-based waits are faster when the app is quick and more reliable when it is slow.
must_not_include: always sleep 10 seconds before every click

cases.question: What artifacts help debug a failed Playwright or Selenium test in CI?
cases.reference_answer: Screenshots, DOM snapshots, video recordings, Playwright traces, browser console logs, and network HAR files help reproduce failures. Attach them on failure in CI so engineers can diagnose without re-running locally first.

cases.question: How do you keep a large UI automation suite maintainable?
cases.reference_answer: Use page objects or screenplay patterns, stable test ids, shared fixtures, and clear data setup/teardown. Tag tests by risk and run smoke on every PR with fuller suites nightly. Quarantine flaky tests with ownership instead of silent retries forever. Review suite health metrics regularly.

cases.question: What is an HTTP 401 status code typically mean?
cases.reference_answer: HTTP 401 Unauthorized usually means the request lacks valid authentication credentials. The client should authenticate or refresh tokens and retry. It is different from 403 Forbidden, which often means identity is known but not allowed.

cases.question: What is contract testing for APIs?
cases.reference_answer: Contract testing verifies that a provider and consumer agree on request/response shapes and semantics, often via schemas or consumer-driven contracts. It catches breaking API changes early without full end-to-end environments for every pair.
must_not_include: contract testing only checks UI pixels

cases.question: How would you test API idempotency for a payment or create endpoint?
cases.reference_answer: Send the same request twice with the same idempotency key or payload and assert only one resource is created and responses are consistent. Verify retries after timeouts do not double-charge. Check status codes and body identity or conflict handling as designed.

cases.question: What security checks belong in basic API regression?
cases.reference_answer: Include authn/authz failures, injection-like inputs, oversized payloads, sensitive data leakage in errors, HTTPS expectations, and rate limiting if specified. Assert no stack traces or secrets in responses. Pair automated checks with periodic deeper security reviews.
must_not_include: security is only a pen-test once a year and never in QA

cases.question: What is a test plan versus a test case?
cases.reference_answer: A test plan describes scope, approach, resources, schedule, and risks for a release or project. A test case is a specific set of steps, data, and expected results for one behaviour. Plans guide strategy; cases execute checks.
must_not_include: plan and case are the same document

cases.question: How should QA write a high-quality bug report?
cases.reference_answer: Include a clear title, environment, steps to reproduce, expected versus actual results, severity, evidence (logs/screenshots), and any workarounds. Note build version and whether the issue is new or regressed. Good reports reduce back-and-forth and speed fixes.
must_not_include: title only is enough for a bug

cases.question: What is shift-left testing?
cases.reference_answer: Shift-left moves testing earlier: reviews, unit tests, API checks, and static analysis during development instead of only after a full build. It finds defects when they are cheaper to fix and improves shared ownership of quality.
must_not_include: shift-left means no testing before release

cases.question: How do you handle a release when critical bugs remain open?
cases.reference_answer: Communicate residual risk clearly with severity, user impact, and likelihood. Agree go/no-go with product and engineering using documented criteria. Consider feature flags, hotfixes, or delayed rollout. Never hide known critical issues; record the decision and follow-up.
must_not_include: ship silently without mentioning known critical bugs

cases.question: What is the role of a test management tool like Azure Test Plans or TestRail?
cases.reference_answer: Test management tools organize cases, plans, runs, and traceability to requirements. They track execution status, ownership, and defects linked to cases. They complement automation rather than replacing it.
must_not_include: automation makes test management tools useless

cases.question: When would you choose Pytest over a pure UI recorder tool?
cases.reference_answer: Pytest suits API, unit, and structured automation with fixtures, parametrization, and CI plugins. Recorders can bootstrap UI flows but often produce brittle scripts. Prefer code-first frameworks for maintainable suites and complex assertions.
must_not_include: recorders are always better than code

cases.question: What is the benefit of structured logging in test automation?
cases.reference_answer: Structured logs with timestamps, case ids, and step markers speed debugging in CI. They help correlate failures with environment issues and support aggregation. Pair logs with screenshots and traces for UI failures.

cases.question: What is exploratory testing?
cases.reference_answer: Exploratory testing is simultaneous learning, test design, and execution guided by charters rather than fully scripted steps. It finds unexpected issues automation misses. Sessions should still capture notes, bugs, and coverage ideas.
must_not_include: exploratory means random clicking with no notes

cases.question: What is non-functional testing? Give examples relevant to web APIs.
cases.reference_answer: Non-functional testing checks how a system behaves, not only what it does: performance, reliability, security, usability, and compatibility. For APIs, examples include load/latency, rate limits, availability, and auth hardening under stress.

cases.question: How would you test an A/B experiment on a checkout page?
cases.reference_answer: Verify assignment logic, both variants render correctly, metrics events fire, and no PII leaks. Test edge cases like refresh, back button, and logged-out users. Coordinate with analytics owners on success metrics and stop conditions if a variant breaks conversion.

cases.question: What does JSON schema validation catch in API tests?
cases.reference_answer: JSON schema validation checks types, required fields, enums, and structure of responses. It fails fast when the contract drifts even if status codes stay 200. It does not fully prove business correctness of values.
must_not_include: schema validation proves all business rules

cases.question: What is test data management and why does it matter?
cases.reference_answer: Test data management is planning creation, isolation, privacy, and cleanup of data used by tests. Poor data causes flakes, false failures, and environment pollution. Prefer fixtures, factories, and dedicated accounts over sharing mutable production-like data blindly.
must_not_include: always use production customer data in tests

cases.question: What is a definition of done for a user story from a QA perspective?
cases.reference_answer: Definition of done should include agreed tests executed, acceptance criteria met, defects of agreed severity resolved or waived, and automation updated when required. It makes quality expectations explicit before a story is called complete.

cases.question: What is mutation testing at a high level?
cases.reference_answer: Mutation testing introduces small code changes (mutants) and checks whether the test suite fails them. If mutants survive, tests may be weak. It measures suite effectiveness beyond line coverage, at higher compute cost.
must_not_include: mutation testing only counts UI clicks

cases.question: How can QA support continuous delivery?
cases.reference_answer: Invest in reliable automation, fast smoke suites, test environments that match production contracts, and clear quality signals in the pipeline. Pair with feature flags and progressive delivery. Focus exploratory effort on high-risk changes each release train.
must_not_include: block every deploy for week-long manual only cycles

cases.question: What is faithfulness in LLM evaluation, and why do QA teams care?
cases.reference_answer: Faithfulness measures whether a model answer stays consistent with provided context instead of inventing facts. QA cares because hallucinations create silent product failures that classical asserts miss. Faithfulness metrics plus golden sets support release gates for RAG and LLM features.
must_not_include: faithfulness means the UI looks pretty
